slides.core_concept: Setting the right frame of mind before training begins
slides.spoken_teaching_script: Welcome. Before we get into any skills, techniques, or scripts — I want you to understand what this program is actually designed to do. Over the next seven days, you are not going to be handed a pitch to memorize. You are going to be built into a professional. Someone homeowners trust within sixty seconds of opening the door. This is an accelerator — which means we compress months of trial and error into one focused week. Every module, every role-play, every simulation is designed to put real confidence in your hands before you ever take your first real appointment.
slides.field_application_example: Think about the difference between a rep who shows up on day one with a script they barely know — and a rep who shows up having already role-played fifty doors, handled every common objection, and practiced their close. That gap is what this seven days is designed to eliminate.
slides.transition_to_next_slide: Before we talk about what you'll be doing, let's talk about what this program can actually do for your income — because knowing the real numbers is one of the most motivating things you'll hear this week.

slides.core_concept: Anchoring realistic income expectations to motivate through the learning curve
slides.spoken_teaching_script: Here's what income looks like in residential solar sales when you do the work. In your first thirty days, most reps earn between three and five thousand dollars. That's the crawling phase — you're figuring out your pitch, reading homeowners, processing rejections. By month three, reps who stayed consistent and kept improving are typically earning eight to twelve thousand dollars per month. By month six and beyond, the top ten percent in this industry are hitting fifteen to twenty-five thousand monthly. Those aren't fabricated numbers — those are real reps who started exactly where you are now. The difference between them and the ones who quit? They understood the curve and pushed through it instead of taking it personally.
slides.field_application_example: When you're knocking your fortieth door of the day and no one's answered, remember this slide. Your income isn't built in one day. It's built in the consistency of showing up day forty-one, day forty-two, day forty-three — until the conversation starts clicking.
slides.transition_to_next_slide: Now you know the destination. Let's look at exactly how we're going to get you there — because the seven-day structure isn't random. Every day builds on the last for a specific reason.

slides.core_concept: Showing the intentional learning arc from foundation to field certification
slides.spoken_teaching_script: Here's how the next seven days are structured — and more importantly, why they're in this order. Day One is foundation — that's today. You're building the mental and technical base that everything else sits on top of. Days Two through Five layer skills progressively: psychology, prospecting, presentation, and closing — in that order, because you can't close what you haven't opened, and you can't open without understanding who you're talking to. Day Six integrates everything into advanced scenarios and your certification exam. And Day Seven is field certification — you'll run a real appointment with a manager observing. By the end of the week, you won't just know what to do. You'll have already done it under pressure.
slides.field_application_example: The reason we end with a live field appointment rather than a written test is simple — knowing the right words and being comfortable saying them to a real person in their living room are two very different things. We train both.
slides.transition_to_next_slide: Before you start learning, there's something important to understand about how learning actually works — because it will explain exactly why some parts of this week feel uncomfortable, and why that discomfort is actually a signal you're growing.

slides.core_concept: Normalizing the discomfort of skill development to prevent early dropout
slides.spoken_teaching_script: Every skill you build in this program follows a predictable four-stage arc. Stage one: you don't know what you don't know. That's where you were before today. Stage two: you start learning and suddenly realize how much there is to master — scripts, objections, personality types, bill analysis. This stage feels overwhelming and that feeling has a name: conscious incompetence. It's uncomfortable, but it means you're paying attention. Stage three: you can execute the skill, but you're still thinking through every step. That's conscious competence — it gets easier every time. Stage four is autopilot. You're in a homeowner's kitchen and the right words just come out naturally because you've practiced them enough times that they live in your muscle memory.
slides.field_application_example: Most reps who quit during their first week quit at stage two — right when it feels hardest. But stage two is not a stop sign. It's a checkpoint. Every top performer you'll ever meet spent real time at stage two before breaking through to the other side.
slides.transition_to_next_slide: Understanding these stages is one thing. Committing to push through all of them is another. And that commitment starts right now with something simple but meaningful — your personal training agreement.

slides.core_concept: Using a personal commitment contract to anchor accountability before training begins
slides.spoken_teaching_script: The last thing you'll do before starting Day One content is sign your commitment agreement. This is not a legal document. It has no financial implications. What it does have is psychological weight — and that matters more than you might think. Research consistently shows that people who write down a commitment and sign it are significantly more likely to follow through on it. You're making a declaration to yourself: that for the next seven days, you'll show up fully, complete every assignment, and hold yourself to the standard of a professional consultant — even on the hard days. Especially on the hard days. Sign it like you mean it.
slides.field_application_example: Top performers in sales treat their daily discipline like a contract. The same way a homeowner signs a contract and commits to going solar — you're committing to showing up as the person you want to become in this career.
slides.transition_to_next_slide: With your commitment signed and your context set, you're ready to begin. Module 1.2 covers your administrative onboarding — and getting these foundational systems right on day one will protect your time and your income for everything that follows.
